clusters.claim: The audit-plus-statutory-deadline design is acceptable only if the deadline is legally binding and actually enforced; an extendable or toothless deadline is equivalent to indefinite reform deferral dressed up as process.

The audit's governance and scope must be genuinely independent — not stacked to pre-determine a case against or for selection, not used to bypass parliamentary or maintainer consultation, and not a backdoor to compel non-state maintainer data-sharing without consent.

Commissioning yet another audit is unnecessary institutionalised delay because a diagnosis and reform pathway are already documented, and every month of unchanged sorting under audit is itself a real cost borne by disadvantaged and Roma pupils, not a neutral wait.

The audit's terms of reference must explicitly cost the teacher-implementation burden (retraining time, workload, transition) with binding union consultation, or any resulting reform risks being administratively elegant but pedagogically unrealistic and unfunded.

An SES-aware, less-coachable admission pilot is defensible only as honest, rigorously monitored evidence-generation on whether the new proxy and assessment actually identify genuine need and aptitude without being gamed, with final criteria published well ahead of the affected application cycle so families and maintainers aren't blindsided.

SES-weighted admission quotas risk displacing genuinely meritorious pupils with an unvalidated, gameable demographic proxy, undermining the meritocratic function of selective tracks; and admission reform alone, without complementary desegregation targets, merely re-labels the queue rather than shortening it.

Any admission pilot must not impose new unfunded workload on teachers or override non-state maintainers' admission discretion — teacher resourcing, union participation in design, and maintainer participation must all be voluntary and negotiated, not mandated.

Every cohort spared early sorting keeps its weakest members alongside stronger peers, so a phased reduction in selective places produces a genuine peer-effect equity gain, provided the pace of reduction does not outrun support for receiving schools.

Phase-down's equity gain is conditional on preventing non-state maintainers from absorbing displaced demand via regulatory levers that do not yet exist or are legally untested against maintainer autonomy, and it risks overcrowding general schools and forcing teacher redeployment unless compensatory capacity funding is guaranteed and disbursed on schedule.

Hungary's documented systemic teacher shortage (OECD, PSZ/PDSZ data) makes the retraining and capacity buildout that phase-down or abolition require unrealistic on the proposed timeline, risking forced redeployment, larger classes, and accelerated attrition unless binding protections (multi-year phase-in, voluntary redeployment with severance, statutory class-size caps) are legislated first.

Abolishing the 6/8-year track is the only scenario that removes the sorting mechanism itself rather than adjusting its dials, matching a documented reform programme's call for unified general education as long as possible.

The equity case for abolition rests on a cross-national association between early tracking and SES gradients rather than Hungary-specific causal evidence, and the scenario's own uncertainty record concedes sorting could simply migrate into school choice, catchment, or non-state tracks — so the reform's full cost could be paid without its promised equity gain.

Abolition overrides parental choice, maintainer autonomy (including church/foundation institutions' 6/8-year formational mission), and an established talent-development pathway, and risks disrupting currently young children's school community and commute unless catchment rules and continuity safeguards are locked in before implementation.

Structural reform risks being reversed once political consensus shifts, as happened in Poland (2016-2019), wasting its equity gains and the government's political capital unless entrenched by a legally binding mechanism stronger than an informal multi-party pact.

Targeted support for general schools (curriculum standards, TEIP-style funding, reduced grade retention) is the only scenario that improves conditions for the majority of pupils without disrupting existing choice, structure, or maintainer arrangements — but it delivers real gains only if the funding is made durable and ring-fenced, since the scenario's own cost analysis flags it as vulnerable to fiscal-consolidation cuts.

Compensating general schools without structural change risks legitimising and permanently entrenching the selective structure's segregating effect rather than fixing it, so it is acceptable only as a time-bounded interim measure with published segregation-reduction targets and a stated structural trajectory, not as a substitute for eventual structural reform.
